name: API and Integration Fundamentals — additive questions
description: Question expansion derived from the attached source files; reuses existing repository topic IDs and defines only missing concepts.

topics.name: CQRS
topics.description: CQRS separates commands that change state from queries that read state. It is useful when read and write models have genuinely different requirements, but it adds synchronization and consistency complexity.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

CQRS separates commands that change state from queries that read state. It is useful when read and write models have genuinely different requirements, but it adds synchronization and consistency complexity.

CQRS means using different models or paths for writing data and reading it, so each side can be optimized for its own job.

## What problem does it solve?

A single model can become awkward when writes enforce rich business rules but reads need denormalized, fast views. Splitting too early, however, creates duplicated models and eventual consistency without enough benefit.

## How it works

An order service validates commands against a transactional write model. An event updates a separate read model that stores the exact shape needed by the customer dashboard.

- Send state-changing intentions through commands
- Validate and persist changes in the write model
- Project changes into one or more read models
- Accept and manage the delay between write and read views

## Common mistakes

- Equating CQRS with using different HTTP methods
- Assuming CQRS always requires event sourcing
- Applying it to a small CRUD service where one model is already clear and fast

## Key terms

- command — an instruction that attempts to change state
- query — a request that reads data without changing state
- projection — a read model built from changes in the write side

topics.name: SOAP Services
topics.description: Contract-first XML web services, their enterprise strengths, and their operational costs.
topics.studyNotes:
## What is it?

How XML envelopes, WSDL contracts, operations, and WS-* standards differ from resource-oriented JSON APIs.

SOAP is a messaging protocol with a formal service contract and standardized extensions, usually transported over HTTP.

## What problem does it solve?

Using SOAP where a simple public JSON API is enough adds heavy tooling, but replacing mature contract and security requirements casually can remove needed guarantees.

## How it works

An enterprise integration generates clients from WSDL and exchanges typed XML envelopes with standardized fault messages.

- WSDL describes operations, messages, endpoints, and data types
- SOAP wraps requests and responses in XML envelopes with headers, body, and faults
- WS-* specifications can add security, reliable messaging, and transactions at significant complexity cost

## Common mistakes

- Calling SOAP merely REST with XML
- Choosing SOAP only for strict typing when simpler schema-based APIs would satisfy the need

## Key terms

- WSDL — A machine-readable SOAP service contract
- envelope — The XML wrapper around a SOAP message
- SOAP fault — A structured error response

questions.title: Applying HTTP Semantics and Retry Safety in practice
questions.prompt: A client sends PUT /users/42 with a complete user representation. The server returns 200 after replacing the resource; repeating the same request leaves the resource in the same state. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Uses methods by meaning
expectedPoints.description: Explains that GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE communicate different intentions rather than being interchangeable.

expectedPoints.label: Explains response semantics
expectedPoints.description: Connects status codes and headers to success, failure, caching, and content negotiation.

expectedPoints.label: Explains idempotency
expectedPoints.description: States why repeated requests matter for retries and distinguishes idempotent effect from safe read-only behavior.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about HTTP Semantics and Retry Safety
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Using POST for every operation because it is convenient." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Uses methods by meaning
expectedPoints.description: Explains that GET, POST, PUT, PATCH, and DELETE communicate different intentions rather than being interchangeable.

expectedPoints.label: Explains response semantics
expectedPoints.description: Connects status codes and headers to success, failure, caching, and content negotiation.

expectedPoints.label: Explains idempotency
expectedPoints.description: States why repeated requests matter for retries and distinguishes idempotent effect from safe read-only behavior.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Returning 200 for failures and hiding the real result only inside the response body?

questions.title: Applying REST Constraints and Resource Modeling in practice
questions.prompt: An order is represented by /orders/731. The client reads it with GET, updates selected fields with PATCH, and cancels it by changing the order state rather than calling a hidden server-side function. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Defines REST correctly
expectedPoints.description: Describes REST as an architectural style based on resources and a uniform interface, not as a protocol or data format.

expectedPoints.label: Explains key constraints
expectedPoints.description: Mentions stateless requests and cacheability, and explains why they help scalability or independent evolution.

expectedPoints.label: Uses a resource model
expectedPoints.description: Shows noun-based resource URIs and maps operations to appropriate HTTP methods.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about REST Constraints and Resource Modeling
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Calling any JSON-over-HTTP API RESTful." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Defines REST correctly
expectedPoints.description: Describes REST as an architectural style based on resources and a uniform interface, not as a protocol or data format.

expectedPoints.label: Explains key constraints
expectedPoints.description: Mentions stateless requests and cacheability, and explains why they help scalability or independent evolution.

expectedPoints.label: Uses a resource model
expectedPoints.description: Shows noun-based resource URIs and maps operations to appropriate HTTP methods.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Putting verbs such as /getOrder or /deleteOrder into every URL?

questions.title: Applying REST vs GraphQL in practice
questions.prompt: A mobile product page needs product data, inventory, reviews, and recommendations. GraphQL can fetch the exact fields in one query, while REST may use several endpoints or a dedicated aggregation endpoint. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Compares data shaping
expectedPoints.description: Explains fixed server responses in REST and client-selected fields in GraphQL, including over- and under-fetching.

expectedPoints.label: Compares operational trade-offs
expectedPoints.description: Discusses HTTP caching and simple observability in REST versus query complexity, N+1, and field-level controls in GraphQL.

expectedPoints.label: Makes a contextual choice
expectedPoints.description: Chooses based on the number and diversity of clients, data graph complexity, and team capability rather than fashion.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about REST vs GraphQL
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Choosing GraphQL only because one endpoint sounds simpler." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Compares data shaping
expectedPoints.description: Explains fixed server responses in REST and client-selected fields in GraphQL, including over- and under-fetching.

expectedPoints.label: Compares operational trade-offs
expectedPoints.description: Discusses HTTP caching and simple observability in REST versus query complexity, N+1, and field-level controls in GraphQL.

expectedPoints.label: Makes a contextual choice
expectedPoints.description: Chooses based on the number and diversity of clients, data graph complexity, and team capability rather than fashion.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Claiming GraphQL automatically removes all over-fetching and latency?

questions.title: Applying RPC vs gRPC in practice
questions.prompt: An internal pricing service exposes CalculateQuote through a .proto contract. Java, Go, and Node.js services generate compatible clients and exchange compact messages over HTTP/2. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the typed contract
expectedPoints.description: Describes .proto definitions, generated stubs, and cross-language compatibility.

expectedPoints.label: Explains transport benefits
expectedPoints.description: Mentions compact binary messages, HTTP/2 multiplexing, and streaming where relevant.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies poor-fit cases
expectedPoints.description: Notes browser, public API, debugging, or human-readability constraints and the cost of another toolchain.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about RPC vs gRPC
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Treating a remote call as reliable and cheap like a local function." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the typed contract
expectedPoints.description: Describes .proto definitions, generated stubs, and cross-language compatibility.

expectedPoints.label: Explains transport benefits
expectedPoints.description: Mentions compact binary messages, HTTP/2 multiplexing, and streaming where relevant.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies poor-fit cases
expectedPoints.description: Notes browser, public API, debugging, or human-readability constraints and the cost of another toolchain.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Changing protobuf field numbers after release?

questions.title: Applying Realtime Delivery Pattern Selection in practice
questions.prompt: A dashboard needs server-to-browser price updates every few seconds but sends commands through normal HTTP. SSE provides a persistent one-way stream, while a chat application would need WebSockets for two-way messages. Would you keep this approach? Explain the trade-offs, likely failure modes, and how you would verify it.
questions.answerStructureHint: Clarify the goal, apply the relevant mechanism, then discuss trade-offs, failure modes, and verification.

expectedPoints.label: Classifies communication direction
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes one-way server push, two-way browser communication, and server-to-server callbacks.

expectedPoints.label: Matches patterns to use cases
expectedPoints.description: Selects SSE for one-way browser updates, WebSocket for interactive two-way traffic, webhooks for server notifications, and polling for simple low-frequency checks.

expectedPoints.label: Discusses operational concerns
expectedPoints.description: Mentions reconnects, heartbeats, ordering, authentication, duplicate delivery, or backpressure as appropriate.

followUps.prompt: Which changed requirement or constraint would reverse your decision?

questions.title: Challenging weak reasoning about Realtime Delivery Pattern Selection
questions.prompt: A teammate claims: "Calling webhooks a browser realtime transport." Challenge the claim. What is wrong or incomplete, and what would you decide instead?
questions.answerStructureHint: Identify the misconception, explain the correct model, and propose a safer practical decision.

expectedPoints.label: Classifies communication direction
expectedPoints.description: Distinguishes one-way server push, two-way browser communication, and server-to-server callbacks.

expectedPoints.label: Matches patterns to use cases
expectedPoints.description: Selects SSE for one-way browser updates, WebSocket for interactive two-way traffic, webhooks for server notifications, and polling for simple low-frequency checks.

expectedPoints.label: Discusses operational concerns
expectedPoints.description: Mentions reconnects, heartbeats, ordering, authentication, duplicate delivery, or backpressure as appropriate.

followUps.prompt: How would you detect this issue in practice: Assuming WebSocket messages are automatically reliable, ordered across reconnects, or replayable?

questions.title: When is CQRS justified?
questions.prompt: What problem does CQRS solve, and what costs would make you reject it for a simple application?
questions.answerStructureHint: Define the split, give a fitting use case, and explain synchronization and consistency costs.

expectedPoints.label: Defines the command-query split
expectedPoints.description: Explains separate write and read models or paths and their different responsibilities.

expectedPoints.label: Identifies a fitting case
expectedPoints.description: Uses CQRS when read and write workloads, shapes, scaling, or business rules differ materially.

expectedPoints.label: Explains the costs
expectedPoints.description: Mentions duplicated models, projection updates, eventual consistency, failure handling, and operational complexity.

followUps.prompt: How would you show the user that a just-submitted change has not reached the read model yet?

questions.title: SOAP and contract-first web services
questions.prompt: What is SOAP, how does WSDL shape development, and when could it be preferable to REST or gRPC?
questions.answerStructureHint: Separate protocol, contract, transport, standardized guarantees, and operational cost.

expectedPoints.label: Defines SOAP and WSDL
expectedPoints.description: Explains XML messaging and the formal machine-readable contract.

expectedPoints.label: Explains standardized capabilities
expectedPoints.description: Mentions faults and relevant WS-* security or reliability extensions.

expectedPoints.label: Makes a contextual choice
expectedPoints.description: Balances enterprise guarantees and interoperability against complexity and payload cost.

followUps.prompt: Why is SOAP a protocol while REST is an architectural style?
